displayTitle: 2025-08-14 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack
topic: Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack
description: Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack Submitters: Melinda McGregor Dept of Internal Affairs (Dog Control) Anthea Oliver, Manager, Operational Policy Karam Talwar, Policy Analyst NZ Veterinary Association Becky Murphy, President, Companion Animal Veterinarians Branch SPCA Dr Alison Vaughan, Scientific Officer
descriptionSummary: Petition of Melinda McGregor: Councils need clear guidelines of required actions following a dog attack. Melinda McGregor. Dept of Internal Affairs (Dog Control)

My name is Melinda McGregor. Um, I'm speaking today on my petition sent to Parliament on the required changes to the dog control laws in New Zealand.

Specifically that councils need clear guidelines of required action following dog attacks. As you would've read in my statement, that my mother was attacked by a dog, and this has led me to be her voice in advocating for change. This is an emotive topic due to the severity of what happened and the after effects.

First and foremost, I want to state that our family are dog lovers and have always had dogs. I will start by giving you a brief picture of what happened, why I believe there needs to be change, and how these changes can be implemented. When we arrived on the scene of the dog attack, the paramedic declared that this wasn't just a normal dog bite.

This was a vicious attack that had gone right through to the bone, leaving three separate puncture wounds on mum's body and a severed finger. The paramedics words were, this dog should be euthanized. This attack had left mum in hospital for two weeks, then on to respite care for a further two weeks.

The aftermath of the dog attack was that the WMA Council contacted me stating they wouldn't be taking this case any further due to mum having dementia, and therefore it wouldn't stand up in court. The council took two drawn out weeks to come up with their judgment not to go ahead with the decision to euthanize.

They did, however, classify the dog as dangerous and the dog was desexed to ask. This wasn't a suitable outcome due to the severity of the incident and the aftermath. The council seemed more concerned with the fact that this was a breeding dog and that the elderly man who was walking the dog was distressed and apologetic.

This was a breeding dog that had, according to the elderly man in charge of the dog at the time, had done this before, but it had not been reported. My thoughts to the council skewed response is that surely this incident shouldn't have needed to go through the court system at all. Clearly it shouldn't matter the victim's age or ability when an attack is of this nature and with medical professionals responding to the fact that the dog was obviously vicious and that it should be euthanized, that surely this should be enough justification for councils

to follow through on. I believe that if there was a standardized scale similar to that of Dr. Ian Dunbar's scale of severity for dog bites, one that is created to be unique to New Zealand, our dog, our council dog control officers would have guidelines to follow.

And this would lead to clarity not only for them, but for victims and dog owners to also know where they stand. The New Zealand Veterinary Association and the SPCA support the introduction of a nationwide stepped approach for managing dog attacks, moving away from the breed specific provisions that are currently in place.

It doesn't matter the breed of the dog, any dog can bite. They go on to say that having this in place would reduce administrative burdens on councils and improve public confidence with current dog control practices, dog control statistics of the tax and destruction orders reported to councils documented from 2018 to 2023.

Um, state there were zero destruction orders from the W Council. Ash Burton Council, her Hui Council, and the Central Otago Council with very low numbers from remaining councils apart from Auckland. This is covering a five year period, which surprises me considering the media coverage of dog attacks that have happened throughout New Zealand.

My key points for change are that council should not have to go through court proceedings when the impact on the victim has been so substantial that they've needed surgery and hospitalization. A nationwide clear mandatory approach for all councils to follow. Using a step to process giving councils clarity in their practice.

My recommendation would be to base this on Dr. Ian Dunbar's dog bite scale categorizing bites into different categories with varying degrees of seriousness. However, the formulation of the scale should include and collaborate with our own dog experts, the Veterinary Association, SPCA and medical professionals to formulate a scale that represents our own requirements.

Inoa, my recommendations would be for all councils to give this out to all owners upon registering their dogs, leaving dog owners with clarity about what happens if their dog was to bite the outcome would have stronger emphasis on the victim's rights and needs for breeders to be subject to checks from authorities, ensuring their dogs are socialized, demonstrate good temperament, and show appropriate behaviors, or before being granted a license to breed.

We are currently relying on decent dog owners to make ethical decisions to make the right call when it shouldn't be left up to them. I support the New Zealand Veterinary Association recommendations to implement their use of their dog scale bite, dog bite scale, rather specifically in their levels five and six. They talk about avoiding court proceedings in these severe cases, therefore reducing trauma for victims and conserving council resources.

The SPCA are also backing the call for a dog by scale to be mandated. They note in their submission to my petition that woefully in 2024, the Supreme Court of New Zealand ruled that a conviction is a precondition to an order for destruction being made under Section 57 3 of the Dog Control Act.

Sharma versus Auckland Council as securing a conviction is a precondition for destruction orders. Now the outcome of this is that counsels may now be more cautious about proceeding with the destruction order. If there are concerns about sufficiency of the evidence or the availability to secure a conviction in court.

This could result in unintended consequences in situations such as when the victim is unable to provide a formal statement or testify as described in my submission. Furthermore, these legal uncertainties often lead to prolonged and distressing court cases and several incidences. Dogs have been held in council for up to two years while legal proceedings are underway.

Prolonged confinement in such environments can severely compromise animal welfare and lead to deterioration behavior. They go on to say that a nationally consistent evidence-based approach is likely to result in a more timely proportionate and humane resolution In these cases, it would also reduce the need for lengthy litigation, protect animal welfare and provide fairer outcomes for all parties involved.

I questioned, did the Supreme Court involve the New Zealand Veterinary Association, the SPCA medical experts in their field and dog attack victims before making such a ruling? I'll conclude my statement by saying Mum was innocently going for a peaceful Sunday walk when she came across this dog. This incident has set her dementia back with her now needing to be in a dementia care home.

She was brave enough to be made by the Yac Council to have to relive the incident being interviewed and recorded in hospital by two council dog control officers. She found the courage to say that it, that it was important to share what had happened to her so that it wouldn't happen again to other people. I feel strongly that what happened to mum and how the council went about it isn't good enough for New Zealand.

We need to hold higher standards of care for human life and wellbeing. Council practices need to change urgently before another type of this attack happens. Again, thank you for your time. Sorry. Um, my name is, uh, Dr. Becky Murphy. So I am a veterinarian, uh, I'm president of the Companion Animal Veterinarian's, uh, special interest branch of the New Zealand Vet Association. So I'm here to support the intent of this petition to amend the dog control act so that every council follows clear, consistent, mandatory guidelines after a dog attack.

As veterinarians, we see both sides of the issue. Every week we see the physical and psychological harm to people, especially children. And we see the dogs involved, the environments they live in and the preventable patterns that lead to incidents. Our message today is simple. We need a modern behavior based stepped framework that councils can apply the same way everywhere with victims at the center and prevention upfront.

Why change? Because the current framework produces different decisions in different districts. It leans too heavily on labels and too lightly on behavior, context and risk. It leaves victims navigating an uncertain process and it doesn't make the best use of what we know works. Education of owners, responsible breeding and socialization, good containment and early intervention when warning signs appear.

So what should the law reflect? First behavior over breed classifications must reflect what happened, the context in which it happened, and the risk of re-offense, not the dog's appearance. Automatic menacing status based on breed sounds decisive, but it's the wrong tool. It's not a reliable predictor of risk. It's difficult to enforce fairly, and it diverts time and money away from the factors that actually reduce harm.

How dogs are managed, socialized, supervised, and, and contained. Focusing on breed can also create a false reassurance. A family hears that their dogs breed as low risk and lets supervision slip or the reverse. A well-managed dog, a well-managed dog is labeled on looks alone and resources that should go into training and containment gets followed by process and dispute.

Meanwhile, the common contributors to bites roaming poor fences, kids and dogs unsupervised together, fear and pain in the dog go unaddressed. Bottom line, a behavior-based approach is more accurate, fairer, and more effective. It targets actual risk in front of us and not stereotypes. So, second, A stepped proportionate response.

Councils need a single national language for describing bite severity and linking the severity to the right action. The most practical way to do that is to adapt an objective bite severity scale. So one best known is the Dr N Dunbar dog bite Scale. As Melinda's already talked about, a classifies incidents by the wounds caused not by the dog's type, which reduces guesswork and helps decisions be consistent and defensible.

So I'll briefly explain in plain language level one's a warning, an ear snap or a display with no skin, no teeth on skin. Level two is teeth on skin, but no puncture. You might see a nick or a scrape. Level three is one to four punctures from a single bite, none deeper than half the length of a canine tooth. You may see a simple tear from someone pulling away.

Level four is more serious, one to four punctures with at least one deeper than half the canine tooth. Often there's bruising from a hard grip and two direction tearing from head shaking. Level five is a multiple by incident with at least two level four injuries or multiple attacks with level four in each level. Six is fatality. Why does it matter?

Because it lets council's tie clear actions to clear descriptions. Most incidents sit at level one and two. These are best served by education and early intervention. Compulsory training with an accredited professional, better containment and supervision, particularly around children. Level three is a step up. It usually responds to targeted training and management, but it shall come with formal conditions and oversight.

Surgical sterilization with loss of gonad hormones, also known as a ectomy, traditionally known as a DS Xing and muzzling in public should be required. Specifying surgical removal of gonad hormones is important because there are several other ways of sterilizing a dog.

Um, if the other techniques don't remove the goad hormones and so you don't get the behavioral benefits from that, which is roaming. And some, some behavior related problems that are related to exogenous sex hormones. Level four is a red flag. Public safety definitely comes first At this level, there should be urgent assessment by an accredited behavior professional or a beha veterinary behaviorist, strict confinement in muzzling while that assessment occurs and a clear enforceable decision based on risk.

Some level four dogs can be safely managed under stringent, enforceable conditions and some cannot. The decision should be evidence based and made very quickly. If the dog is not euthanized, then he should be surgically sterilized with removal of gonadal hormones and muscle in public. As with the level three, levels five and six are extreme Risk council should have immediate authority to act to protect the public supported by veterinary or medical evidence.

So it brings me to the third principle competent assessment where behavioral assessment is required on a dog. It must be done by people with the right training skills and ethical standards. Accredited professionals using humane evidence-based methods or veterinary behaviorist council should know exactly who they can call and the public should know exactly what that assessment involves.

As clarity builds trust companion animals, New Zealand has a robust system to accredit such people and could be used in these situations. The fourth principle put victims at the center and get serious about data. Victims deserve a name, contact set timeframes for updates and clear information on what happens next, how long decisions will take, what the options are, and where to go to for medical and a CC support.

At the same time, every council should record incidents in a common way and contribute to the, to a national data set without good data on context, bite level, contributing factors and outcomes. We can't target prevention or evaluate what's working. So how does this align with the petition before you? The NCVA is fully aligned on the goal clear mandatory national guidelines.

So councils act consistently and communities are safer. We support a behavior-based multi-tier framework anchored in an objective by scale with victim-centered obligations and national reporting. There's just one refinement, one difference. The petition contemplates immediate euthanasia for level four bites.

We understand why level four injuries are serious and frightening, and public safety is paramount. Our recommendation is safety first and still proportionate. Require urgent assessment by an accredited professional or a veterinary behaviorist under strict confinement and muzzling before a final decision is made. Some level four dogs cannot be made safe and should not return to the community and others can be made low risk under enforceable conditions.

A short expert assessment makes that clear and keeps the public safe while it happens for levels five and six where injuries are multiple and severe, or whether there has been a fatality, council should have immediate authority to act supported by clinical evidence to protect the public and to minimize further trauma to victims. So what should be in the act at a high level by our opinion, require a national bite level classification for every reported incident Require councils to link each level to define powers, timeframes,

and owner obligations require that when an assessment is needed, it is performed by an accredited professional Using humane. Evidence-based methods require a victim care pathway with mandated contact points and access to decisions require national data and annual reporting. So we can learn target prevention and measure progress require owner and breeder licensing to reflect on dog ownership as a privilege and the importance of early socialization and adult behavioral outcomes.

These aren't theoretical changes. They're practical, implementable and already used in parts of the sector. They will reduce serious harm, shorten decision timeframes, and increase public confidence. Most importantly, it will focus attention where it belongs on behaviors and environments we can change. So I'll end where I begin. Dogs are a valued part of life in our country.

Most are well loved and well managed, but when things go wrong, the consequences can be devastating. We owe it to victims and to the wider public to respond with clarity, consistency, and compassion. And we owe it to dogs to make decisions based on evidence and risk and not on labels. A behavior-based stepped framework grounded in a, an objective by scale, delivered by accredited professionals paired with victim centered practice.

And national reporting can make our communities and dogs safer. The veterinary profession stands ready to help with technical details and to support councils to make this work on the ground. Um, I think I have presented some of you already in previous petitions, but I am Dr. Allison Vaughn. I'm presenting on behalf of the SPCA and I've been a scientific officer with SPCA for over seven years and have a background in applied animal behavior. We are concerned SPCA with the welfare of dogs and other animals, but also with promoting responsible dog ownership.

And we work closely with councils up and down the country to address shared concerns regarding dogs. Um, as you've said, you have our written submissions, so I will briefly focus on some key points. Firstly, the Dog Control Act is outdated. This act is over 30 years old and it has had only minor amendments, um, and hasn't had a substantive review, um, since it was introduced.

As a result, it hasn't kept pace with modern science or best practice. And the result is this inconsistency across councils. So right now, a similar instance can be treated very differently depending on where they occur. And this is stressful for victims, confusing for dog guardians and undermines public trust.

Now dog bites are an emotive issue and this is justifiably so these incidents can be traumatic for those involved and they also create a lot of political and community pressure for action. This is really commendable, but is important that we don't miss opportunities when taking action in terms of amending legislation.

And it's really important that when we do change legislation, the responses are grounded in evidence. And so we're actually introducing something that will be effective in reducing dog by incidents in New Zealand. I, on that note, would like to commend the petitioner, um, for advocating for an evidence-based proportionate response that would both improve how we deal with dog bite incidents and address the current postcode lottery we have across New Zealand.

In terms of cancel responses, we support the petition's call for a nationally mandated stepped response framework based on evidence.
